original_hypothesis.title: WebAuthn Signature Forgery via Spoofed RIP-7212 Precompile on Non-Supporting Chains
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: WebAuthn.verify performs a staticcall to address 0x100 (the RIP-7212 precompile) before falling back to FCL. The check only verifies `ret.length > 0` and `abi.decode(ret, (uint256)) == 1`. On chains where a regular contract is deployed at address 0x100 (not the legitimate precompile), if that contract returns 32 bytes containing uint256(1) for arbitrary inputs, ALL passkey signature verifications would pass without actual cryptographic validation. This would allow any attacker to impersonate any passkey owner and drain all wallet funds.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The wallet targets 8+ chains (Ethereum, Base, Optimism, Arbitrum, Polygon, BNB, Avalanche, Gnosis). Address 0x100 content varies across chains. If any current or future target chain has non-precompile code at this address, the entire passkey authentication is bypassed.
deployment_assumptions: Address 0x100 on all target chains is either empty or the legitimate RIP-7212 precompile
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: WebAuthn.sol line 18: `address private constant VERIFIER = address(0x100)` - hardcoded without chain-specific validation

proof_of_signal.pattern: staticcall to VERIFIER (0x100) with only length check `ret.length > 0` and decode check `== 1`, no validation that response is from legitimate precompile

preconditions: Contract deployed on a chain where address 0x100 has non-precompile code
Code at 0x100 returns 32 bytes containing uint256(1) for staticcall inputs
Wallet has at least one passkey owner

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Precompile fallback logic at lines 145-153 trusts any contract at hardcoded address
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - purely dependent on chain environment
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent on any chain with spoofable 0x100 address

operational_questions: What code exists at address 0x100 on each of the 8 target chains?
Could future chain upgrades or forks deploy code at 0x100?
Does the staticcall result in valid-looking but incorrect verification on any chain?
validation_requirements: Audit address 0x100 on all target chains
Test WebAuthn.verify with arbitrary r,s values when 0x100 returns uint256(1)
Verify FCL fallback is never reached when 0x100 returns truthy data

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a target chain where address 0x100 has non-precompile code
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Verify that code at 0x100 returns 32 bytes with uint256(1) for arbitrary inputs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Craft a WebAuthnAuth struct with arbitrary r,s values (no valid signature needed)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Submit UserOp or ERC-1271 signature impersonating any passkey owner to drain wallet

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete wallet takeover for any wallet using passkey owners
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires emergency upgrade to fix WebAuthn verification logic
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All signature validations for passkey owners

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - understanding of WebAuthn and precompile mechanics
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Exploitable immediately and permanently on affected chains

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Successful passkey verifications with random r,s values
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Drain transactions from wallets with passkey-only owners

validation_result.mechanism: Precompile spoofing via hardcoded address trust
validation_result.impact_summary: On any chain where address 0x100 contains a non-precompile contract returning uint256(1), all WebAuthn/passkey signature verifications pass without cryptographic validation, enabling complete wallet takeover.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The WebAuthn.verify function performs a staticcall to a hardcoded address 0x100 (the RIP-7212 precompile) and trusts the response if it has length > 0 and decodes to uint256(1). The code's sole defense is checking `ret.length > 0` and `abi.decode(ret, (uint256)) == 1`. There is no validation that address 0x100 actually contains the legitimate RIP-7212 precompile versus an arbitrary contract.

The attack flow:
1. On a chain where 0x100 has a contract that returns uint256(1) for any input
2. Attacker constructs a valid-looking WebAuthnAuth struct with correct type string, matching challenge (which they can compute from the known message hash), UP flag set, and arbitrary r,s values
3. The pre-verification checks (type, challenge, UP flag) all pass because the attacker controls clientDataJSON and authenticatorData
4. The staticcall to 0x100 returns uint256(1), bypassing actual ECDSA verification
5. FCL fallback is never reached because `success && valid` is true

This is a genuine code defect: the code makes an unverified environmental assumption about address 0x100. While the FCL fallback was designed for chains without the precompile (where 0x100 has no code and staticcall returns empty data), the code provides no protection against a chain where 0x100 has non-precompile code that returns favorable data.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal references are accurate. WebAuthn.sol line 18 defines `address private constant VERIFIER = address(0x100)` with no chain-specific validation. The verify function's precompile call logic (approximately lines 145-153 based on the source) performs only a length check and uint256(1) comparison on the return data, with no mechanism to authenticate that the responder is the legitimate RIP-7212 precompile.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The code trusts any contract at hardcoded address 0x100 that returns 32 bytes decoding to uint256(1). On chains where 0x100 has non-precompile code returning this value, the FCL fallback (actual cryptographic verification) is completely bypassed. The attacker can forge any passkey signature with arbitrary r,s values.
Hardcoded address with no chain-specific validation or configuration mechanism. The same address is trusted across all deployment chains regardless of what code (if any) exists there.

calling_sequence: 1. EntryPoint calls CoinbaseSmartWallet.validateUserOp() or external caller calls isValidSignature()
2. _validateSignature() is called with message hash and signature
3. SignatureWrapper decoded; owner bytes loaded by ownerIndex
4. If ownerBytes.length == 64 (passkey owner), WebAuthn.verify() is called
5. WebAuthn.verify checks s <= P256_N_DIV_2 (attacker sets valid s)
6. WebAuthn.verify checks type field in clientDataJSON (attacker constructs valid JSON)
7. WebAuthn.verify checks challenge in clientDataJSON (attacker computes correct challenge from known message hash)
8. WebAuthn.verify checks UP flag in authenticatorData (attacker sets bit)
11. If spoofed contract at 0x100 returns uint256(1): success=true, ret.length=32, valid=true
12. abi.decode(ret, (uint256)) == 1 returns true, function returns true
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is step 10-13. When 0x100 has a contract that returns uint256(1) for any input, the staticcall succeeds with ret.length > 0. The condition `success && valid` is true, so the code returns `abi.decode(ret, (uint256)) == 1` which is true. The FCL fallback (actual cryptographic verification) at step 13 is bypassed entirely. The attacker only needs to pass the pre-ECDSA checks (steps 5-8), all of which they can satisfy since they control clientDataJSON and authenticatorData.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Address 0x100 on all target chains is either empty or the legitimate RIP-7212 precompile
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The foundry.toml configuration contains no chain-specific settings, no target chain list, and no validation of address 0x100. There are no deployment scripts provided that specify target chains or verify precompile availability. The code hardcodes VERIFIER = address(0x100) without any runtime or deploy-time chain validation.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is feasible on any chain where address 0x100 has a non-precompile contract that returns uint256(1). The attacker: (1) identifies a target wallet with a passkey owner on such a chain, (2) constructs a WebAuthnAuth struct with the correct challenge derived from the target message hash, correct type field, UP flag set, and arbitrary r,s values where s <= P256_N_DIV_2, (3) submits either a UserOp or ERC-1271 signature, (4) the spoofed 0x100 returns uint256(1), bypassing ECDSA verification, (5) the wallet accepts the forged signature and executes arbitrary operations. The main limitation is finding a chain where 0x100 has such a contract. On current major chains (Ethereum, Base, Optimism, Arbitrum), 0x100 is either empty (safe fallback to FCL) or the legitimate precompile. However, the code provides no protection if this assumption is violated on future chains or edge-case deployments.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL if exploitable on a target chain. Complete bypass of passkey signature verification enables: wallet takeover, arbitrary fund transfers, owner manipulation, and implementation upgrades. All wallets using passkey owners on the affected chain would be vulnerable. However, exploitation probability is limited by the environmental requirement of a specific contract at 0x100.
recommended_mitigations: Add chain-specific configuration for the RIP-7212 verifier address instead of hardcoding
Include a registry or allowlist of chains known to support RIP-7212 and skip the precompile call on unsupported chains
Add a secondary validation: after getting uint256(1) from 0x100, optionally verify with FCL as well (double-check mode)
Use a constructor or initializer parameter to set the verifier address per deployment, with validation
Consider checking code size at 0x100 against known precompile code hash if feasible
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a mock contract at address 0x100 on a local fork that returns abi.encode(uint256(1)) for any staticcall input. 2. Deploy CoinbaseSmartWallet with a passkey owner. 3. Construct a WebAuthnAuth struct with valid type/challenge fields but random r,s values. 4. Call isValidSignature or submit a UserOp with the forged signature. 5. Verify that the signature is accepted without valid ECDSA signing.
uncertainties: Whether any current target chain has non-precompile code deployed at address 0x100
Whether future target chains might have non-precompile code at 0x100
Whether chain operators could be socially engineered to deploy code at 0x100
The practical difficulty of deploying a contract at exact address 0x100 on a permissionless chain
additional_testing_needed: Audit address 0x100 on all 8+ target chains for existing code
Test WebAuthn.verify on a chain where 0x100 returns constant uint256(1)
Evaluate whether CREATE/CREATE2 can practically target address 0x100 on any chain
Monitor address 0x100 across target chains for future code deployment
